<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Computability theory</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

← Notebook
1 Turing machines 2 Recursive functions 3 Halting 4 Rec vs r.e. 5 Reductions 6 Gödel

Computability theory

What can a machine compute, and what is permanently out of reach? From Turing's tape and Kleene's recursion schemes to the diagonal that kills the halting problem, the gap between recursive and recursively enumerable, Rice's theorem, and the computability lens on Gödel — six concepts that fence off the reachable from the unreachable.

An algorithm is something we recognise when we see it, but pinning down the notion turns out to be the deepest move in 20th-century logic. Turing's tape, Church's $\lambda$-calculus, and Kleene's $\mu$-recursion all converged on the same class of partial functions $\mathbb{N}^k\rightharpoonup\mathbb{N}$. The Church–Turing thesis is the empirical claim that any reasonable model of computation lands at the same place. Once you accept this, a single counting argument — there are countably many algorithms but uncountably many functions — already guarantees most functions are not computable. The sharper question is which specific problems are out of reach: halting, equivalence, satisfiability, Hilbert's tenth, and (via reduction) most of the natural problems about programs themselves.

Plan. Section 1 fixes a model — the Turing machine — and Section 2 shows it agrees with recursion-theoretic schemes. Section 3 runs Cantor's diagonal against the universal simulator to kill halting. Sections 4–5 unpack the consequences: r.e. but not recursive sets, the many-one degree structure, Rice's theorem. Section 6 ports halting into arithmetic to recover Gödel's incompleteness theorems.

blocks.html:
1. Turing machines

A (deterministic, single-tape) Turing machine is a tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{\text{acc}}, q_{\text{rej}})$ where $Q$ is a finite state set, $\Sigma\subset\Gamma$ are the input and tape alphabets (the blank $\sqcup\in\Gamma\setminus\Sigma$), and

\[ \delta\colon Q\times \Gamma \to Q\times\Gamma\times\{L,R\} \]

tells the machine, given its current state and the symbol under the head, which symbol to write, which way to move the head, and which state to enter next. A configuration is the triple $(q, T, h)$ recording state, full tape contents, and head position. The machine starts at $q_0$ with the input written on the leftmost cells and blanks elsewhere; it accepts if it reaches $q_{\text{acc}}$, rejects at $q_{\text{rej}}$, and loops if it never halts.

Robustness of the model. Multitape, two-counter, two-stack, RAM, and nondeterministic TMs all simulate one another with at most polynomial overhead. The class of computable functions is the same. This empirical convergence is the content of the Church–Turing thesis: any reasonable formalism for "effective procedure" yields exactly this class.

The widget below steps a tiny TM that increments a binary number written least-significant-bit-first. Each press of step applies one transition; watch the head sweep, find the rightmost 0 (or run off the end), flip it to 1, and clear the trailing 1's on the way back.

Turing machine: binary increment
step through a TM adding 1 to a binary number (LSB-first)

Used in
Computability theory · Recursive and partial-recursive functions
Computability theory · The halting problem

<script>
/* =========================== Turing-machine widget =========================== */
(function(){
  const svg=$('#tm-svg'), out=$('#tm-out'), inSel=$('#tm-input');
  const stepBtn=$('#tm-step'), runBtn=$('#tm-run'), resetBtn=$('#tm-reset');
  // Tape uses '_' for blank.
  // States: q0 (carry, scanning right), q1 (scanning right past 0/1 to find rightmost), q2 (return to start), qH (halt).
  // We use a simpler design: scan right to first blank, then move left, flipping 1->0 with carry, until we hit a 0 (set to 1) or a blank (write 1).
  const TRANS = {
    // [state, read] -> [write, move, nextState]
    'q0,0': ['0','R','q0'],
    'q0,1': ['1','R','q0'],
    'q0,_': ['_','L','q1'],
    'q1,0': ['1','L','q2'],   // found first 0 from right -> set to 1, done carrying
    'q1,1': ['0','L','q1'],   // 1 + carry = 0, carry continues left
    'q1,_': ['1','S','qH'],   // ran off the left end -> write a new leading 1
    'q2,0': ['0','L','q2'],
    'q2,1': ['1','L','q2'],
    'q2,_': ['_','R','qH']    // returned to the left edge -> done
  };
  const W=620, H=220, CELL=44, CELLS=11;
  let tape=[], head=0, state='q0', halted=false, history=[];

  function loadInput(){
    const s = inSel.value;
    tape = ['_','_'];                 // pad left with blanks
    for(const c of s) tape.push(c);
    while(tape.length < CELLS+2) tape.push('_');
    head = 2;                          // first input cell
    state = 'q0';
    halted = false;
    history = [];
  }

  function step(){
    if(halted) return;
    const a = tape[head] || '_';
    const key = state+','+a;
    const t = TRANS[key];
    if(!t){ halted=true; return; }
    const [w, mv, ns] = t;
    history.push({pos:head, was:a, write:w, mv, from:state, to:ns});
    tape[head] = w;
    if(mv==='L') head -= 1;
    else if(mv==='R') head += 1;
    if(head<0){ tape.unshift('_'); head=0; }
    while(head >= tape.length) tape.push('_');
    state = ns;
    if(state==='qH') halted = true;
  }

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    // determine viewport: show CELLS cells centered on head where possible
    const left = Math.max(0, head - 5);
    const padX = (W - CELLS*CELL)/2;
    const tapeY = 90;
    // tape cells
    for(let i=0;i<CELLS;i++){
      const idx = left + i;
      const x = padX + i*CELL;
      const sym = tape[idx] ?? '_';
      const isHead = (idx === head);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{
        x, y:tapeY, width:CELL, height:CELL, rx:4,
        fill: isHead ? 'rgba(255,216,102,0.12)' : 'var(--panel2)',
        stroke: isHead ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--line)',
        'stroke-width': isHead ? 2 : 1
      }));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:x+CELL/2, y:tapeY+CELL*0.62,
        'text-anchor':'middle', 'font-size':18, 'font-weight':600,
        fill: sym==='_' ? 'var(--mute)' : 'var(--ink)'});
      t.textContent = sym==='_' ? '␣' : sym;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // head pointer (triangle above active cell)
    const hx = padX + (head - left)*CELL + CELL/2;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{
      d:`M${hx-9} ${tapeY-6} L${hx+9} ${tapeY-6} L${hx} ${tapeY+4} z`,
      fill:'var(--yellow)'
    }));
    // state badge
    const stateColor = halted ? 'var(--green)' : (state==='q1' ? 'var(--pink)' : (state==='q2' ? 'var(--cyan)' : 'var(--blue)'));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:30, y:30, width:140, height:36, rx:6,
      fill:'var(--panel2)', stroke:stateColor, 'stroke-width':2}));
    const st = SVG('text',{x:100, y:54, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':16,
      'font-weight':700, fill:stateColor});
    st.textContent = halted ? 'q_H (halted)' : state;
    svg.appendChild(st);
    // legend / role hint
    const hintY = 175;
    const role = halted
      ? 'finished — tape holds (input + 1)'
      : (state==='q0' ? 'q₀: scan right looking for the right-edge blank'
        : state==='q1' ? 'q₁: walk left carrying — flip 1→0, stop when 0 becomes 1'
        : 'q₂: walk left back to the start, no more flips');
    const r = SVG('text',{x:W/2, y:hintY, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,
      fill:'var(--mute)','font-style':'italic'});
    r.textContent = role; svg.appendChild(r);
    // tape index ticks
    for(let i=0;i<CELLS;i++){
      const idx = left + i;
      const x = padX + i*CELL + CELL/2;
      const t = SVG('text',{x, y:tapeY-14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,
        fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = idx;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // readout
    const tapeStr = tape.map((c,i)=> i===head ? `[${c==='_'?'␣':c}]` : (c==='_'?'␣':c)).join(' ');
    const last = history[history.length-1];
    const lastStr = last ? `last: ${last.from},${last.was==='_'?'␣':last.was} → ${last.write==='_'?'␣':last.write},${last.mv},${last.to}`
                          : 'no transitions yet';
    out.textContent =
      `state : ${state}${halted?'  (halted)':''}\n` +
      `head  : ${head}\n` +
      `tape  : ${tapeStr}\n` +
      `${lastStr}\n` +
      `steps : ${history.length}`;
  }

  stepBtn.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ step(); render(); });
  runBtn.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    let guard = 200;
    while(!halted && guard-- > 0) step();
    render();
  });
  resetBtn.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ loadInput(); render(); });
  inSel.addEventListener('change', ()=>{ loadInput(); render(); });
  loadInput(); render();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Recursive and partial-recursive functions

Turing's tape is one model; Kleene's recursion schemes are another. The class of partial recursive functions $\mathbb{N}^k\rightharpoonup\mathbb{N}$ is the smallest collection containing the basic functions:

  • Zero $Z(n) = 0$.
  • Successor $S(n) = n+1$.
  • Projections $P_i^k(x_1,\ldots,x_k) = x_i$.

and closed under three operations:

  • Composition. If $g\colon\mathbb{N}^m\to\mathbb{N}$ and $h_1,\ldots,h_m\colon\mathbb{N}^k\to\mathbb{N}$ are recursive, so is $g(h_1(\vec x),\ldots,h_m(\vec x))$.
  • Primitive recursion. Define $f$ by $f(\vec x, 0) = g(\vec x)$ and $f(\vec x, n+1) = h(\vec x, n, f(\vec x, n))$.
  • Unbounded minimisation ($\mu$). $f(\vec x) = \mu y\,[g(\vec x, y) = 0]$ — the least $y\ge 0$ with $g(\vec x, y) = 0$, undefined if no such $y$ exists.

Without the $\mu$-operator you get the primitive-recursive functions — every member is total, and addition, multiplication, exponentiation, and bounded search all live here. With $\mu$ you reach partial functions and exact agreement with Turing-computability.

Why $\mu$ is the bridge. Primitive recursion can simulate any bounded search, but Turing machines can run forever. The $\mu$-operator is the recursion-theoretic "while-loop": ask $g(\vec x,0), g(\vec x,1),\ldots$ until a zero appears, and accept partiality if no zero ever does. Ackermann's function is total and TM-computable but not primitive-recursive — you need $\mu$ (or a TM, equivalently) to define it cleanly.

The widget runs three small recursion schemes — the predecessor (primitive recursion), addition (primitive recursion), and a μ-search for the least $y$ with $y^2 \ge n$. Watch the recursion stack unwind for the primitive cases; for $\mu$, watch the linear scan halt as soon as the predicate fires.

Recursion-scheme tracer
step a primitive recursion or a $\mu$-search

<script>
/* =========================== Recursion-scheme tracer =========================== */
(function(){
  const svg=$('#rec-svg'), out=$('#rec-out'), which=$('#rec-which');
  const aIn=$('#rec-a'), bIn=$('#rec-b'), go=$('#rec-go');

  function tracePred(n){
    // pred(0)=0 ; pred(n+1)=n. Pure primitive recursion.
    const frames = [];
    if(n===0){ frames.push({label:`pred(0)`, val:0, depth:0}); return frames; }
    // unwind: pred(n) = h(n-1, pred(n-1)) but we only need step depth 1
    frames.push({label:`pred(${n})`, val:null, depth:0});
    frames.push({label:`= h(${n-1}, pred(${n-1}))`, val:null, depth:1});
    frames.push({label:`= ${n-1}`, val:n-1, depth:1});
    return frames;
  }
  function traceAdd(m, n){
    // add(m,0)=m ; add(m,n+1) = S(add(m,n)).
    const frames = [];
    for(let k=n;k>=0;k--){
      frames.push({label: k===0 ? `add(${m},0) = ${m}` : `add(${m},${k}) = S(add(${m},${k-1}))`,
        val: null, depth: n - k});
    }
    // unwinding pass
    let acc = m;
    for(let k=1;k<=n;k++){
      acc = acc + 1;
      frames.push({label:`= ${acc}  (after ${k} successor)`, val:acc, depth: n - 1 + k});
    }
    return frames;
  }
  function traceMu(n){
    // mu y . y*y >= n.
    const frames = [];
    let y = 0;
    while(y*y < n && y < 50){
      frames.push({label:`y=${y}: ${y}² = ${y*y} < ${n}, continue`, val:null, depth:y});
      y++;
    }
    frames.push({label:`y=${y}: ${y}² = ${y*y} ≥ ${n}  ✓`, val:y, depth:y});
    return frames;
  }

  function render(frames){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    const W=620, H=240, padL=30, padT=20;
    const lh = 22;
    frames.slice(0, Math.floor((H-2*padT)/lh)).forEach((f, i)=>{
      const y = padT + i*lh;
      const xIndent = padL + Math.min(f.depth, 8) * 14;
      // marker dot
      const isResult = (f.val !== null);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:xIndent-10, cy:y-4, r:3,
        fill: isResult ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--violet)'}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:xIndent, y, 'font-size':13,
        fill: isResult ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--ink)',
        'font-family':'ui-monospace,monospace',
        'font-weight': isResult ? 700 : 400});
      t.textContent = f.label;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // legend
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:W-180, cy:H-18, r:4, fill:'var(--violet)'}));
    const t1 = SVG('text',{x:W-170, y:H-14,'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t1.textContent = 'recursive call';
    svg.appendChild(t1);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:W-90, cy:H-18, r:4, fill:'var(--green)'}));
    const t2 = SVG('text',{x:W-80, y:H-14,'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t2.textContent = 'returned value';
    svg.appendChild(t2);
  }

  function runIt(){
    const a = Math.max(0, Math.min(20, parseInt(aIn.value,10)||0));
    const b = Math.max(0, Math.min(20, parseInt(bIn.value,10)||0));
    let frames, summary;
    if(which.value==='pred'){
      frames = tracePred(a);
      bIn.disabled = true;
      summary = `pred(${a}) = ${Math.max(0,a-1)}\nscheme : primitive recursion (no $\\mu$)\ntotal? : yes`;
    } else if(which.value==='add'){
      frames = traceAdd(a, b);
      bIn.disabled = false;
      summary = `add(${a},${b}) = ${a+b}\nscheme : primitive recursion (h = S ∘ proj)\ntotal? : yes`;
    } else {
      frames = traceMu(a);
      bIn.disabled = true;
      const y = Math.ceil(Math.sqrt(a));
      summary = `μy.[y² ≥ ${a}] = ${y}\nscheme : unbounded minimisation\ntotal? : yes here, but μ in general can diverge`;
    }
    render(frames);
    out.textContent = summary;
  }

  go.addEventListener('click', runIt);
  which.addEventListener('change', runIt);
  aIn.addEventListener('change', runIt);
  bIn.addEventListener('change', runIt);
  runIt();
})();
</script>
</section>

3. The halting problem

Once we have a notion of computation, the natural meta-question is: can a program decide whether another program halts? Encode every Turing machine $M$ as a string $\langle M\rangle$ and form the language

\[ H \;=\; \{\,\langle M, w\rangle : M \text{ halts on input } w\,\}. \]

Suppose, for contradiction, $h$ is a TM that decides $H$: it always halts and accepts iff $\langle M,w\rangle\in H$. Build a new machine $D$ that on input $\langle M\rangle$:

  1. Runs $h$ on $\langle M, \langle M\rangle\rangle$.
  2. If $h$ says "halts", $D$ enters an infinite loop. If $h$ says "doesn't halt", $D$ halts.

What does $D$ do on its own code $\langle D\rangle$? If $D$ halts on $\langle D\rangle$, then $h$ on $\langle D,\langle D\rangle\rangle$ said "halts", so $D$ is supposed to loop — contradiction. If $D$ loops, $h$ said "doesn't halt", so $D$ should halt — contradiction. Either way we lose, so no such $h$ exists.

Theorem (Turing 1936). The halting problem $H$ is undecidable: no Turing machine decides it.

The structure of the proof is Cantor's diagonal applied to the countable list of TMs. The widget below shows that diagonal explicitly: rows are machines $M_0, M_1, M_2,\ldots$, columns are inputs $0, 1, 2, \ldots$, and a check mark means "this machine halts on that input". Toggle the diagonal to see how $D$ disagrees with each $M_i$ on input $i$ — and is therefore not on any row.

Diagonal table for halting
click a diagonal cell to flip it; D's row is the negation of the diagonal

See also
  • Naive set theory · Countability

Used in
Computability theory · Gödel incompleteness — computability lens
Computability theory · Recursive vs recursively enumerable
Computability theory · Undecidability via reduction

<script>
/* =========================== Diagonal-table widget =========================== */
(function(){
  const svg=$('#diag-svg'), out=$('#diag-out');
  const N = 6;
  // halts[i][j] = does M_i halt on input j?
  // start with a deterministic-looking pattern so diagonal is non-trivial.
  let halts = Array.from({length:N},(_,i)=> Array.from({length:N},(_,j)=> ((i*3+j) % 5) < 3));
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    const W=620, H=320;
    const padL=110, padT=40, cell=44;
    // column headers (inputs)
    for(let j=0;j<N;j++){
      const t = SVG('text',{x:padL+cell/2+j*cell, y:padT-14, 'text-anchor':'middle',
        'font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = `w=${j}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // row labels + cells
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      const ty = padT + cell/2 + i*cell + 5;
      const lbl = SVG('text',{x:padL-12, y:ty, 'text-anchor':'end',
        'font-size':13, fill:'var(--violet)','font-weight':600});
      lbl.textContent = `M_${i}`;
      svg.appendChild(lbl);
      for(let j=0;j<N;j++){
        const x=padL+j*cell, y=padT+i*cell;
        const onDiag = (i===j);
        const v = halts[i][j];
        svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x, y, width:cell, height:cell, rx:4,
          fill: onDiag ? 'rgba(255,216,102,0.16)' : 'var(--panel2)',
          stroke: onDiag ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--line)',
          'stroke-width': onDiag ? 2 : 1,
          style: onDiag ? 'cursor:pointer' : '',
          'data-i': onDiag ? String(i) : ''
        }));
        const m = SVG('text',{x:x+cell/2, y:y+cell*0.62,
          'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':16,'font-weight':700,
          fill: v ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--pink)',
          'pointer-events':'none'});
        m.textContent = v ? '✓' : '✗';
        svg.appendChild(m);
      }
    }
    // D row, immediately below M_{N-1}
    const dy = padT + N*cell + 8;
    const lbl = SVG('text',{x:padL-12, y:dy+cell/2+5,'text-anchor':'end',
      'font-size':13, fill:'var(--cyan)','font-weight':700});
    lbl.textContent = 'D';
    svg.appendChild(lbl);
    for(let j=0;j<N;j++){
      const x=padL+j*cell, y=dy;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x, y, width:cell, height:cell, rx:4,
        fill:'var(--panel2)', stroke:'var(--cyan)','stroke-width':1.5}));
      const dv = !halts[j][j];
      const m = SVG('text',{x:x+cell/2, y:y+cell*0.62,
        'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':16,'font-weight':700,
        fill: dv ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--pink)'});
      m.textContent = dv ? '✓' : '✗';
      svg.appendChild(m);
    }
    // legend (viewport coords)
    const lY = H - 8;
    const items = [['var(--green)','✓ halts'], ['var(--pink)','✗ loops'],
      ['var(--yellow)','diagonal (click)'], ['var(--cyan)','D = ¬diagonal']];
    items.forEach((p,k)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:30+k*150, y:lY-12, width:14, height:14, rx:3,
        fill:'transparent', stroke:p[0], 'stroke-width':1.5}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:48+k*150, y:lY-1,'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = p[1]; svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // readout
    const diag = halts.map((row,i)=> row[i] ? '✓' : '✗').join(' ');
    const drow = halts.map((row,i)=> row[i] ? '✗' : '✓').join(' ');
    let mismatch = -1;
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){ if((!halts[i][i]) !== (!halts[i][i])) {} }
    out.textContent =
      `diagonal (M_i on i) : ${diag}\n` +
      `D's row (¬diagonal) : ${drow}\n` +
      `\nFor every i, D disagrees with M_i on input i — so D ≠ M_i for all i.\n` +
      `Yet D was supposed to be in the list of all TMs. Contradiction.\n` +
      `\nclick any yellow diagonal cell to flip M_i(i); D's row updates`;
  }
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const idx = e.target && e.target.getAttribute && e.target.getAttribute('data-i');

  });

4. Recursive vs recursively enumerable

The halting problem is undecidable, but it is not "infinitely far" from being decidable. It is semi-decidable: simulate $M$ on $w$, and if it halts, accept; if it doesn't, you wait forever — but you do correctly classify every member of $H$. This is the next layer up.

A set $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ is recursive (decidable) if its characteristic function $\chi_A$ is computable; recursively enumerable (r.e., semi-decidable, $\Sigma_1^0$) if there is a TM that halts exactly on inputs in $A$. Equivalently: $A$ is the range of a (partial) computable function, or $A = \{x : \exists y\,T(e, x, y)\}$ for some Turing-machine code $e$ and Kleene's $T$-predicate.

Post's theorem. $A$ is recursive iff both $A$ and $\mathbb{N}\setminus A$ are r.e. Proof. If both have semi-deciders, dovetail them on each input — exactly one halts, giving a decider. Conversely a decider yields semi-deciders for both.

So the gap "recursive ⊊ r.e." is exactly the gap between r.e. and co-r.e. The halting set $H$ is r.e. (just simulate); not recursive (Turing's theorem); and its complement $\overline H$ is not even r.e. (a separate diagonal).

5. Undecidability via reduction

Reductions are the engine for spreading undecidability from one problem to another. For sets $A, B\subseteq\mathbb{N}$, write

\[ A \le_m B \quad\text{iff}\quad \exists\,\text{computable }f\colon\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\text{ with } x\in A \iff f(x)\in B. \]

Equivalently: a black-box decider for $B$ would, via $f$, decide $A$. The point is the contrapositive: if $A$ is undecidable and $A\le_m B$, then $B$ is undecidable too. Hardness travels upward.

Rice's theorem. Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a non-trivial property of r.e. languages (some TM has it, some doesn't). Then $\{\langle M\rangle : L(M)\in\mathcal{P}\}$ is undecidable. Sketch. WLOG $\emptyset\notin\mathcal{P}$; pick $M_0$ with $L(M_0)\in\mathcal{P}$. Given $\langle M,w\rangle$, build $M'$ that on input $x$ first simulates $M$ on $w$ (ignoring output), then runs $M_0$ on $x$. Then $L(M') = L(M_0)$ if $M$ halts on $w$, else $\emptyset$. So $\langle M'\rangle\in\mathcal{P}$ iff $\langle M,w\rangle\in H$ — a halting reduction.

The map $H\le_m B$ is the swiss-army knife. Use it to kill TM-emptiness, TM-equivalence, totality, regularity, context-freeness — and via more delicate reductions, Hilbert's tenth problem (Diophantine solvability), the word problem for finitely presented groups, and Post's correspondence problem.

6. Gödel incompleteness — computability lens

Gödel's incompleteness theorems (1931) predate Turing by five years, but in hindsight the cleanest proof routes through halting. Fix a consistent, recursively axiomatised theory $T$ extending Robinson arithmetic $\mathsf{Q}$ — strong enough to express bounded computation, weak enough that we know what its proof-search looks like.

Inside $T$ we can encode Turing machines, configurations, and runs as natural numbers (Gödel numbering). The statement "$M$ halts on $w$" becomes a $\Sigma_1$ formula:

\[ \mathrm{Halt}(\ulcorner M\urcorner, \ulcorner w\urcorner) \;\equiv\; \exists t\, \mathrm{Halts\text{-}by\text{-}step}(\ulcorner M\urcorner,\ulcorner w\urcorner, t). \]
First incompleteness theorem. If $T$ is consistent and recursively axiomatised, $T$ is incomplete: there is a true $\Sigma_1$ sentence (about halting) that $T$ neither proves nor refutes.

Why: if $T$ proved every true halting fact and refuted every false one, we could decide halting by alternating proof-search on $\mathrm{Halt}$ and $\neg\mathrm{Halt}$ — contradicting Turing. The "true but unprovable" sentence Gödel produces is exactly such a missing halting fact, dressed up via the diagonal lemma so it self-references its own unprovability.

Rosser's refinement. Gödel's original proof needed $\omega$-consistency. Rosser modified the self-referential sentence to "for every proof of me there is a shorter refutation", so the conclusion goes through with plain consistency.

The second incompleteness theorem formalises the first inside $T$: from $\mathrm{Con}(T)$ one can derive (within $T$) the unprovability of the Gödel sentence $G$, hence $G$ itself — contradicting $T\not\vdash G$. So $T\not\vdash\mathrm{Con}(T)$.

7. Connections

Once you accept the Church–Turing thesis, "what can be computed" stops being a soft question — it has a sharp answer, and the sharpness is what makes the rest of the theory work.

Complexity theory

Computability says whether a problem can be solved at all. Complexity theory says how efficiently — the same diagonal argument that gives the halting problem also gives the time / space hierarchy theorems.

First-order logic and Gödel

First-order logic + Gödel-arithmetisation reduces "$T$ proves $\varphi$" to a recursively enumerable but not decidable predicate; the halting problem reappears as the consistency-strength comparison theorem.

Type theory and proofs as programs

Type theory via Curry–Howard makes computability and provability the same activity: a term inhabiting type $A$ both witnesses the truth of $A$ and computes a value of type $A$.

Algorithms and number theory

Hilbert's tenth problem (Diophantine equations are undecidable) connects computability to number theory; analogously, the word problem in groups is undecidable for finitely-presented groups but decidable for hyperbolic groups.

Open frontiers: Kolmogorov complexity and Chaitin's $\Omega$ (incompleteness via algorithmic randomness); higher computability (recursion in higher types, hyperarithmetic / analytical hierarchy); reverse mathematics; computational interpretations of constructive proofs.
